focus: R vs L contrast + Affricates (/tʃ/ /dʒ/) + Diphthongs (/aɪ/ /aʊ/ /ɔɪ/)
focus_ar: التمييز بين R و L + الحروف المركبة + الحروف المتحركة المزدوجة
arabic_challenge: R/L confusion is the #1 pronunciation issue for Arabic speakers. Arabic /r/ is a trill (tongue tip vibrates). American /r/ is retroflex (tongue curls back without touching anything). Arabic /l/ is similar to English but Arabic speakers often substitute their /r/ where English has /l/ and vice versa.

isolation.instructions: CRITICAL DIFFERENCE: /l/ = tongue TIP touches the ridge behind upper teeth. /r/ = tongue curls BACK — does NOT touch anything. Your tongue tip points toward the roof but stays suspended in mid-air.
isolation.instructions_ar: الفرق الحاسم: /l/ = طرف اللسان يلمس الحافة خلف الأسنان العلوية. /r/ = اللسان يرجع للخلف — ما يلمس أي شي! طرف اللسان يتجه لسقف الفم بس يبقى معلّق بالهواء.
practice: ra ra ra (tongue curls BACK, doesn't touch)
isolation.mirror_check: Use a mirror: for /l/, you can see your tongue tip touching behind your teeth. For /r/, your tongue tip is hidden (curled back).

sentence_practice: I really like reading in my room.
The long road leads to the restaurant on the left.
Look right, then look left before you cross.
daily_drills.record_this: I really like learning languages. The road is long but I love the ride. I look at the light on the right. I read and I listen. I learn and I grow.
daily_drills.self_check: For /r/: your tongue should NOT touch anything inside your mouth. If it touches, that's /l/ or an Arabic /r/. American /r/ = tongue floats backward. Practice in a mirror.

daily_drills.focus: R and L in the MIDDLE and END of words (harder than initial position)

isolation.instructions: R and L at word END: /l/ ('feel') = tongue goes UP to touch roof. /r/ ('fear') = tongue curls BACK. Listen for the difference in the vowel quality too — vowels before /r/ sound 'colored'.
isolation.instructions_ar: R و L في نهاية الكلمة أصعب: /l/ (feel) = اللسان يرتفع. /r/ (fear) = اللسان يرجع.

sentence_practice: I feel like calling my family after work.
The world is beautiful from here.
Where are the other people? They are all in the hall.
daily_drills.record_this: I feel really good this morning. I arrived here early. The world looks beautiful after the rain. I want to call my family. All of them are far from here. I live near the school. The water is cold but I don't care.
daily_drills.self_check: Record 'feel' and 'fear' back to back. They should sound CLEARLY different. If they sound the same, focus on tongue position: UP (l) vs BACK (r).

isolation.instructions: /tʃ/ (chair, which) = tongue tip to roof, release with 'sh' sound. Like t+sh combined. /dʒ/ (job, age) = same position + voice. Like d+zh combined.
practice: j j j (like the start of 'jam')

sentence_practice: Each teacher chooses which language to teach.
I enjoy my job — I just joined last June.
Watch the children in the kitchen change the channel.
daily_drills.record_this: I just changed my job in June. My teacher is rich and generous. I enjoy watching the children choose lunch. Each age brings a new challenge. Which language do you like?
daily_drills.self_check: For /tʃ/: start with your tongue touching the roof (like /t/) then release into /ʃ/ (sh). It's a TWO-PART sound.

isolation.instructions: Diphthongs are MOVING sounds (two vowel positions in one syllable). /aɪ/ (my, like) = mouth open → closes upward. /aʊ/ (how, now) = mouth open → lips round. /ɔɪ/ (boy, join) = lips round → spread.
isolation.instructions_ar: الأصوات المزدوجة: يبدأ الصوت في مكان وينتهي في مكان ثاني. /aɪ/ = أه→إي. /aʊ/ = أه→أو. /ɔɪ/ = أو→إي.

sentence_practice: I like my nice house in town.
How about going out now? I found my toy!
The boy enjoys the noise. It's his choice.
daily_drills.record_this: I like my life right now. Five times I tried to find my voice. The boy pointed down the road. How about we go out? I enjoy the sound of the night. It's time to join.
daily_drills.self_check: Diphthongs must MOVE. Say 'my' — does your mouth start open and close? Say 'how' — do your lips start open and round at the end? If the sound is flat/stable, it's wrong.

daily_drills.focus: Putting it all together — R and L before/after diphthongs
sentence_practice: Turn right at the traffic light.
I like riding around the town at night.
The loud noise came from the round tower on the right.
The royal family is loyal and proud.
daily_drills.record_this: I ride my bike around town. I turn right at the light. The road is long but I like it. The loud boys are playing outside. I enjoy this time — night time is the right time. I am loyal to my choice.
daily_drills.self_check: In 'right' — is the /r/ different from 'light'? Record both and compare.

challenge_sentences: I really love learning languages. It's my choice.
The children are playing loudly around the large room.
Right now I enjoy watching the night sky from my rooftop.
Join me — let's ride our bikes around the town.
daily_drills.record_this: I really love my life right now. Learning English is my choice. I enjoy riding around town — the roads are long but beautiful. Last night I joined my friends. The children were playing loudly. It was really nice.
daily_drills.self_check: Count every R and L in your recording. Are they ALL distinct? Focus especially on: really, right, around, long, love, learning, loudly.

test_yourself.passage: I really like my life right now. I live in a large house near a beautiful river. Every morning I ride my bike around the town. I look right, then left. The road is long but I enjoy the ride. Last night I joined my friends at a restaurant. The children were playing loudly. We had a choice: watch a movie or listen to music. I chose to listen. The royal concert was loud and joyful. Learning English is my choice — I love it.

sounds_to_check./r/ vs /l/: really, right, river, ride, around, road, restaurant, royal — like, live, large, look, left, long, loudly, listen, loud, love, learning
sounds_to_check./tʃ/ vs /dʒ/: children, choice, chose, watch — joined, joyful, enjoy
sounds_to_check.Diphthongs: like(/aɪ/), life(/aɪ/), right(/aɪ/), ride(/aɪ/), night(/aɪ/) — around(/aʊ/), house(/aʊ/), loud(/aʊ/) — joined(/ɔɪ/), joyful(/ɔɪ/), choice(/ɔɪ/)

scoring_guide.90_100: Clear R/L distinction in all positions. Diphthongs move properly. Affricates crisp.
scoring_guide.70_89: R/L mostly distinct. Occasional confusion in final position (feel/fear). Diphthongs good.
scoring_guide.50_69: R/L confusion persists in several words. This is NORMAL for week 4 — keep practicing.
scoring_guide.below_50: R still sounds like Arabic trilled /r/ or like /l/. Spend extra time on Day 1-2 drills.

daily_drills.encouragement: R/L is the HARDEST distinction for Arabic speakers. If you scored 50-69%, you are on track. It takes most learners 4-8 weeks of daily practice to make this automatic. Don't give up — every day of practice builds the new neural pathway.
